one_line_takeaway: WebWeaver shows that LLM-MAS communication topology -- a claimed high-value IP and safety-relevant asset -- can be recovered with near-perfect accuracy from a single compromised non-administrative agent using only contextual/stylometric cues (no agent IDs), evading keyword-based defenses that fully neutralize prior topology-leakage attacks, via a combination of a trained sender predictor plus adaptive GCG jailbreaks or, when jailbreaks fail, a jailbreak-free masked-diffusion graph-completion fallback.
key_finding: Sender predictor performance (Table 1, Precision/Recall/F1): CSQA 0.8794/0.8773/0.8772, GSM8k 0.9471/0.9472/0.9470, Fact 0.9606/0.9597/0.9597, Bias 0.9830/0.9830/0.9830 -- all above 0.85 F1. Graph completion (Table 2): jailbreak-based module reaches Precision 1.0000 on all four datasets (F1 0.8666-1.0000, MRR 0.7292-1.0000, e.g. perfect 1.0/1.0/1.0/1.0 on Fact and Bias); the jailbreak-free diffusion module trails but stays competitive (F1 0.7887-0.8198, MRR 0.7029-0.8659). Under a keyword-based defense that strips agent-ID references (Figure 3), the IP Leakage baseline collapses to near-zero across all metrics, CZRL retains only moderate performance, while WebWeaver's jailbreak-free ablation alone already outperforms both baselines, and the full jailbreak-based WebWeaver achieves the highest precision/recall -- underlying the paper's headline claim of 'about 60% higher inference accuracy under active defenses' versus SOTA. Scalability: F1 stays high from 5 to 20 agents across all four datasets (Figure 4), and attack efficacy on Llama-3-70B is comparable to or better than the 7B-12B cluster (Appendix A.4.2), attributed to stronger instruction-following sharpening each agent's stylometric/semantic fingerprint. Overhead (Table 3, seconds): e.g. on Bias, WebWeaver's jailbreak-based module costs 15.4s offline / 15.1s online vs. IP Leakage's 9.9s offline / 54.4s online, while the jailbreak-free variant ('w/o') incurs zero measured online overhead. Ablations: the proposed gradient-free sender-predictor ensemble matches BERT-based encoding on most datasets (Table 5, e.g. 0.9/0.9/0.9 on GSM8k/Fact/Bias) while being cheaper; GCG-optimized jailbreak prompts substantially beat a static 'Fixed' prompt against LlamaGuard (Table 4, e.g. F1 1.00 vs 0.69 on Fact, 1.00 vs 0.58 on Bias). Per-topology results (Table 6, 200 simulations) show centralized/dense topologies (Star, Complete) are consistently more vulnerable to all three methods than sparse ones (Chain) -- e.g. under CSQA, WebWeaver F1 is 0.87 on Chain vs 0.93 on Complete -- and WebWeaver dominates both CZLR and IP Leakage baselines across every topology/dataset combination tested.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is WebWeaver, an attack framework that infers the complete communication topology of an LLM-based multi-agent system (LLM-MAS) by compromising only a single arbitrary (non-administrative) agent, using two complementary modules: (1) a jailbreak-based module that trains a 'sender predictor' on collected inter-agent dialogues to de-anonymize message senders from content alone (no agent IDs needed), then uses a recursive propagation prompt escalated to a GCG-optimized adversarial suffix to make neighboring agents forward their conversation histories, recursively expanding the inferred graph; and (2) a jailbreak-free fallback module that reframes topology completion as conditional graph generation via a masked DDPM diffusion model, with a novel 'Manifold-Constrained Fusion' masking strategy (with a proof in the appendix) that preserves the known/observed subgraph during the reverse diffusion process while inpainting unknown edges. This is distinguished by the authors from prior work (Wang et al. 2025a, 'IP Leakage attacks targeting LLM-based multi-agent systems'), which is cited as the only directly relevant baseline and which the paper claims relies on unrealistic assumptions (control of the administrative agent, and direct identity queries via jailbreaks that keyword-based defenses can trivially block).
threat_model: Attacker's goal: infer the full communication topology of a target LLM-MAS, treated as high-value IP, in order to enable stronger downstream attacks and IP theft. Attacker's ability: controls a single arbitrary (non-administrative) agent embedded in the target system (k=1 of N agents compromised, with N/2 as the stated general upper bound on k, though only k=1 is evaluated) and can proactively request context retransmission from adjacent agents (framed as plausible given asynchronous/multi-device operation); the attacker also knows which agents participate in the target system and can independently collect its own inter-agent dialogue corpus (e.g., from a separate LLM-MAS it controls, motivated by the example of inter-university research collaborations) to train the sender predictor offline. No white-box access to victim agents is assumed -- the GCG suffix optimization is computed against a local proxy LLM and then transferred to the target.
limitations: The authors' own stated limitation: due to ethical/practical concerns (consent, privacy, interference with ongoing collaborations), the method was not evaluated on real-world online academic-collaboration platforms or live collaborative projects -- evaluation is confined to controlled, reproducible experimental settings, with real-world validation left to future work requiring domain-specific coordination with research teams. Additional caveats visible in the paper's own text (not authors' explicit 'Limitations' section but load-bearing for interpreting results): the threat model requires the attacker to already know which agents participate in the target system and to be able to independently collect a labeled dialogue corpus to train the sender predictor offline -- a nontrivial precondition not evaluated for sensitivity (e.g., domain mismatch between the attacker's own collected corpus and the victim system). GCG suffix optimization is computed on a local proxy model without white-box access to the victim, so transfer success is not separately quantified beyond the LlamaGuard ablation (Table 4). Only k=1 compromised agent is evaluated experimentally even though the threat model formally allows up to k=N/2. Practical deployments are noted (by the authors, citing prior work) to typically use only 2-10 agents, so the 5-20 agent scalability sweep exceeds but does not stress-test far beyond realistic system sizes.

datasets_used: CSQA (CommonsenseQA, Talmor et al. 2019) -- 12,247 multiple-choice commonsense questions
GSM8k (Cobbe et al. 2021) -- 8,500 grade-school math word problems
Fact (Yu et al. 2024, NetSafe) -- synthetic factual statements generated via GPT-4
Bias (Yu et al. 2024, NetSafe) -- stereotype-based statements for safety/alignment evaluation
quantitative_results: Sender predictor F1 (Table 1): CSQA 0.8772, GSM8k 0.9470, Fact 0.9597, Bias 0.9830

Jailbreak-based graph completion (Table 2): Precision 1.0000 on all 4 datasets; F1 0.8666 (CSQA), 0.9821 (GSM8k), 1.0000 (Fact), 1.0000 (Bias); MRR 0.7292 (CSQA), 0.9208 (GSM8k), 1.0000 (Fact), 1.0000 (Bias)
Headline claim: WebWeaver achieves approximately 60% higher inference accuracy than SOTA baselines under active (keyword-based) defenses, with negligible overhead (Abstract, Introduction, Conclusion)
Overhead in seconds, offline/online (Table 3), e.g. Bias dataset: IP Leakage 9.9/54.4; WebWeaver 15.4/15.1; jailbreak-free variant reports no online cost
Jailbreak-prompt ablation vs LlamaGuard (Table 4), F1: Fixed prompt 0.64 (CSQA)/0.56 (GSM8k)/0.69 (Fact)/0.58 (Bias) vs. GCG-optimized 0.72/0.93/1.00/1.00
Sender-predictor architecture ablation (Table 5): proposed gradient-free ensemble matches BERT F1 on GSM8k/Fact/Bias (0.9/0.9/0.9) and is close on CSQA (0.8 vs 0.8)

Per-topology results over 200 simulations (Table 6): WebWeaver F1 by topology on CSQA -- Chain 0.87, Star 0.92, Tree 0.83, Complete 0.93, MRR 0.78; on Bias -- Chain 0.94, Star 0.96, Tree 0.85, Complete 0.96, MRR 0.89. CZLR baseline MRR stays in 0.54-0.67 range; IP Leakage baseline F1 near 0.00-0.05 across all topologies/datasets
Scalability: F1 remains high as agent count scales from 5 to 20 (Figure 4); attack efficacy on Llama-3-70B is comparable to or exceeds the 7B-12B model cluster (Appendix A.4.2)
baselines_compared: IP Leakage (Wang et al. 2025a) -- the only prior work directly targeting LLM-MAS topology confidentiality; relies on controlling the administrative agent and direct jailbreak-based identity queries

CZRL (Fang et al. 2025) -- contrastive zero-shot relational learning for knowledge-graph completion, adapted as a graph-completion baseline (assumes attacker can access other agents' dialogues via jailbreak to learn text embeddings per node)
